w:r.w:t: SpanForge-Core
Learning Curriculum
Complete Table of Contents
April 2026 • Confidential
Parts
Chapters
~120 hrs
Estimated study time
Concept tracks
About this curriculum

SpanForge-Core is a compliance-grade AI observability SDK that covers an unusually broad range of computer science and engineering concepts: cryptographic audit chains, distributed tracing, privacy regulation, compliance engineering, SDK architecture patterns, enterprise security, and production testing. Built in 2 months with AI-assisted development, it represents a condensed reference implementation across all of these domains.

This curriculum organises every concept in the codebase into 8 parts and 47 chapters, structured to build understanding progressively — from the core event model through to enterprise deployment and supply chain security. Each chapter includes 3 specific learning points grounded in the actual SpanForge implementation.
Concept tracks
Six colour-coded concept tracks run across the curriculum. Each chapter is tagged with its primary track:
core
Event model, tracing, exporters
security
Cryptography, auth, secrets
compliance
PII, regulations, evidence chains
ops
Observability, cost, drift, alerts
advanced
SDK architecture, enterprise
testing
Testing, DX, toolchain
Suggested learning tracks
Learners can follow the full curriculum in order, or take a focused track based on their role:
Track
Parts covered
Audience
Security engineering track
Part 2 + Part 7 §7.1–7.7
Security engineers, platform engineers
Compliance & privacy track
Part 3 + Part 5
Compliance officers, legal, data engineers
Observability track
Part 1 + Part 4
SRE, DevOps, platform teams
SDK architecture track
Part 6 + Part 8
Senior engineers, library authors
Enterprise deployment track
Part 7 (all sections)
Enterprise architects, infrastructure leads
Full curriculum
Parts 1–8 in order
All engineers joining the SpanForge project
Parts 1 and 2 are prerequisites for all tracks. The event model (Part 1) and security primitives (Part 2) underpin every other module in the codebase.
Part 1
core
Foundations — how SpanForge works
chapters
~12 hrs
est. time

Everything in SpanForge rests on a small number of carefully designed primitives: the event envelope, the ULID, the tracer, and the batch exporter. Understanding these well means understanding every module that builds on them.
What is AI observability and why it matters
core
The RFC-0001 event envelope — schema, fields, versioning
core
ULID — lexicographically sortable IDs without coordination
core
The event type registry — 74 canonical types across 17 namespaces
core
Traces, spans, and the context propagation model
core
The tracer, the @trace decorator, and span lifecycle hooks
core
The batch exporter — buffering, backpressure, and circuit breaking
core
Sampling strategies — TraceIdRatio, ParentBased, AlwaysOn
core
Chapter details
What is AI observability and why it matters
The difference between logging, monitoring, and observability in LLM systems
Why standard APM tools miss LLM-specific failure modes (hallucination, cost drift, PII leakage)
How SpanForge positions itself against Langfuse, Arize, and Helicone
The RFC-0001 event envelope — schema, fields, versioning
Schema version negotiation between producers and consumers
Mandatory fields, optional extensions, and the reverse-domain namespace requirement for third-party types
__slots__ and MappingProxyType — why immutability matters for HMAC stability
ULID — lexicographically sortable IDs without coordination
Why UUIDs are wrong for distributed event systems and ULIDs are right
Crockford Base32 alphabet, O(1) decode table, and I/L/O lookalike rejection
threading.Lock for monotonicity across runtimes — not just CPython GIL
The event type registry — 74 canonical types across 17 namespaces
How namespaces are structured: llm.trace.*, llm.cost.*, llm.guard.*, decision.*, consent.*
Why EventType is a str subclass rather than an Enum
Third-party extension types and the reverse-domain prefix requirement
Traces, spans, and the context propagation model
contextvars vs. thread-local — why contextvars is the right choice for asyncio
W3C TraceContext and Baggage propagation across service boundaries
Parent-child span relationships and how they survive across await points
The tracer, the @trace decorator, and span lifecycle hooks
How the module-level tracer singleton is initialised and configured
Using @trace for automatic span creation around any function
Registering on_llm_call and on_agent_end hooks for cross-cutting concerns
The batch exporter — buffering, backpressure, and circuit breaking
Why synchronous export blocks are wrong for production SDKs
The background thread model, queue depth, and overflow handling
Per-exporter circuit breakers — 5 failures → OPEN, 30s auto-reset
Sampling strategies — TraceIdRatio, ParentBased, AlwaysOn
Head-based sampling and its tradeoffs vs. tail-based sampling
always_sample_errors=True — why you never want to drop error spans
Configuring sample_rate per project without code changes
Part 2
security
Security primitives — cryptography you can trust
chapters
~18 hrs
est. time

This part covers the cryptographic foundations of SpanForge — HMAC chains, key management, JWT authentication, TOTP, and secrets scanning. Every concept here is implemented in pure stdlib with no external cryptographic dependencies.
HMAC-SHA256 signing — how and why every event is signed
security
Tamper-evident audit chains — chain linkage, prev_id, and tombstones
security
Key rotation — rolling keys without breaking the chain
security
GDPR tombstone erasure — right to erasure in an immutable log
security
API key lifecycle — format, scoping, rotation, and revocation
security
JWT authentication — HS256 vs RS256, JWKS, and token introspection
security
TOTP / MFA — RFC 6238, backup codes, and brute-force lockout
security
Secrets scanning — 20 patterns, Shannon entropy, SARIF output
security
Timing-safe comparisons and SecretStr — writing code that doesn't leak
security
SSRF protection — why IP allowlists alone are not enough
security
Chapter details
HMAC-SHA256 signing — how and why every event is signed
Why HMAC over digital signatures for audit chains — performance and key management
The deterministic to_json() with sort_keys=True that makes HMAC stable
hmac.compare_digest() — timing-safe comparison and why string equality is not
Tamper-evident audit chains — chain linkage, prev_id, and tombstones
How prev_id links events into a verifiable chain
verify_chain() — O(n) verification and what a broken chain tells you
Tombstone events for GDPR erasure — erasing content without breaking the chain
Key rotation — rolling keys without breaking the chain
The key_map and rotation boundary verification pattern
5-minute overlap window during rotation — why this is necessary
Emitting CHAIN_ROTATED audit events at rotation boundaries
GDPR tombstone erasure — right to erasure in an immutable log
Why you cannot simply delete from an HMAC chain
The tombstone pattern — what it stores, what it erases, what it proves
How erasure receipts satisfy GDPR Article 17 requirements
API key lifecycle — format, scoping, rotation, and revocation
sf_live_<48-char-base62> format — why format validation at parse time matters
Scoping: pillar_whitelist, project_scope, ip_allowlist, expires_at
5-minute overlap window during rotation — safe key handover
JWT authentication — HS256 vs RS256, JWKS, and token introspection
Why HS256 is fine for local mode and RS256 is required for production
JWKS endpoint caching with 1-hour TTL — balancing freshness and performance
jti claim and revocation lists — invalidating tokens before expiry
TOTP / MFA — RFC 6238, backup codes, and brute-force lockout
RFC 6238 TOTP: SHA-1, 6 digits, 30s period, ±1 time-step drift tolerance
Backup codes as SHA-256 hashes only — plaintext never retained after enrolment
5 wrong OTPs → 30-minute lockout — implementing brute-force protection correctly
Secrets scanning — 20 patterns, Shannon entropy, SARIF output
Why pattern order matters — specific before generic prevents false suppression
Shannon entropy scoring — bits/char, the 3.5 threshold, and context keyword windows
SARIF 2.1.0 output format and GitHub Code Scanning integration
Timing-safe comparisons and SecretStr — writing code that doesn't leak
Timing attacks on string comparison and why == is dangerous for secrets
SecretStr wrapper — __repr__ = '<SecretStr:***>' and exception sanitisation
The rule: secrets never in logs, __repr__, or exception messages
SSRF protection — why IP allowlists alone are not enough
Server-Side Request Forgery in export pipelines — how it happens
Why the SSRF check in SpanForge explicitly notes that DNS hostnames are not covered
Defence-in-depth: IP literal checks, URL allowlists, and egress enforcement
Part 3
compliance
Privacy & PII — compliance-by-design
chapters
~14 hrs
est. time

PII protection in production AI systems is not just about regex patterns. This part covers the full stack from detection through redaction, the five-level sensitivity model, multi-regulation compliance, and the consent boundary enforcement that ties it all together.
PII detection — regex vs. Presidio NLP vs. hybrid approaches
compliance
Sensitivity levels — from public to critical, and the 5-tier model
compliance
Redaction policies — flag, redact, block, and the audit trail they create
compliance
Luhn, Verhoeff, and SSN validation — structured PII verification
compliance
GDPR Article 17, CCPA DSAR, HIPAA Safe Harbor — regulation-specific patterns
compliance
India DPDP Act — patterns, consent enforcement, and data residency
compliance
Consent boundaries — granting, revoking, and emitting consent.violation events
compliance
Differential privacy — Laplace noise on numeric quasi-identifiers
compliance
Chapter details
PII detection — regex vs. Presidio NLP vs. hybrid approaches
When regex is enough and when you need NLP entity recognition
Microsoft Presidio AnalyzerEngine — 18 entity types including IN_AADHAAR, MEDICAL_LICENSE, NRP
Graceful fallback when presidio-analyzer is not installed
Sensitivity levels — from public to critical, and the 5-tier model
PUBLIC → INTERNAL → CONFIDENTIAL → SENSITIVE → CRITICAL
How sensitivity level determines the redaction policy applied
Luhn algorithm and Verhoeff algorithm for structured number validation
Redaction policies — flag, redact, block, and the audit trail they create
flag: detect and annotate without modifying — when to use it
redact: replace with [REDACTED:pii] and inject audit metadata into the payload
block: raise SFPIIBlockedError — stopping the pipeline on high-sensitivity hits
Luhn, Verhoeff, and SSN validation — structured PII verification
Luhn algorithm — credit card number validation and how check digits work
Verhoeff algorithm — why it catches more transposition errors than Luhn
SSN range validation — why format alone is insufficient for US Social Security numbers
GDPR Article 17, CCPA DSAR, HIPAA Safe Harbor — regulation-specific patterns
GDPR Article 17 Right to Erasure — erase_subject() and the ErasureReceipt
CCPA DSAR — export_subject_data() and what must be included in a data access request
HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification — 18 PHI identifiers per 45 CFR §164.514(b)(2)
India DPDP Act — patterns, consent enforcement, and data residency
The DPDP_PATTERNS dictionary — Aadhaar, PAN, Voter ID, Passport, bank account formats
SFPIIDPDPConsentMissingError — raised when DPDP entity detected without valid consent
Data residency routing — IN region enforcement at the SDK layer
Consent boundaries — granting, revoking, and emitting consent.violation events
ConsentBoundary as a first-class module — not an afterthought
grant_consent() / revoke_consent() / check_consent() — the three operations
How consent.violation events flow into the HMAC audit chain
Differential privacy — Laplace noise on numeric quasi-identifiers
What differential privacy guarantees and what it does not
The Laplace mechanism — epsilon, sensitivity, and the noise scale
When to apply DP noise and when anonymisation is sufficient
Part 4
ops
Observability — making AI systems legible
chapters
~16 hrs
est. time

Observability for LLM systems requires more than standard APM. This part covers OpenTelemetry alignment, the full export backend stack, cost tracking, drift detection, and the alert routing system that makes all of it actionable.
OpenTelemetry alignment — OTel Semantic Conventions for GenAI v1.27+
ops
W3C TraceContext and Baggage — propagating context across service boundaries
ops
Export backends — OTLP, Datadog, Grafana Loki, Splunk, Elastic
ops
WORM append-only storage — S3 Object Lock, GCS, Azure Immutable Blob
ops
Cost tracking — per-model pricing, BudgetMonitor, and budget alerts
ops
Drift detection — behavioural baselines, distribution stats, and alert thresholds
ops
Alert routing — topic-based publish, dedup, escalation, and on-call integration
ops
Metrics extraction — latency percentiles, p95, and aggregation from traces
ops
The span processor pipeline — enrichment, redaction, and custom processors
ops
Debug tooling — print_tree, visualize(), Gantt timelines, and the trace store
ops
Chapter details
OpenTelemetry alignment — OTel Semantic Conventions for GenAI v1.27+
gen_ai.system, gen_ai.request.model, gen_ai.usage.input_tokens — the correct attribute names
Why OTel-first matters: vendor portability and the cost of proprietary span formats
Migrating non-conforming span attributes to OTel Semantic Conventions
W3C TraceContext and Baggage — propagating context across service boundaries
traceparent and tracestate headers — format, parsing, and propagation
W3C Baggage for cross-cutting context: project_id, domain, tier
How context propagation links SpanForge spans to downstream service calls
Export backends — OTLP, Datadog, Grafana Loki, Splunk, Elastic
OTLP dual routing — events with trace_id → spans, events without → log records
Datadog, Grafana Loki, Splunk HEC, Elastic ECS — format translation patterns
Per-exporter circuit breakers — a failed Datadog sink does not block OTLP export
WORM append-only storage — S3 Object Lock, GCS, Azure Immutable Blob
fsync after every write, O_APPEND semantics, threading.Lock — why all three are needed
Automatic rotation with CHAIN_ROTATED audit events at rotation boundaries
S3 COMPLIANCE mode Object Lock vs. GOVERNANCE mode — when each is required
Cost tracking — per-model pricing, BudgetMonitor, and budget alerts
The _pricing.py table — how per-model token costs are maintained
CostTracker: breakdown_by_model(), total_usd(), call_count() aggregates
BudgetMonitor: threshold callbacks and the budget_alert() convenience factory
Drift detection — behavioural baselines, distribution stats, and alert thresholds
BehaviouralBaseline construction from LLM span events via from_events()
DistributionStats: mean, stddev, p95, p99 — lightweight without NumPy
AMBER and RED drift thresholds — configuring per-metric sensitivity
Alert routing — topic-based publish, dedup, escalation, and on-call integration
Topic-based routing vs. direct sink calls — why the indirection matters
5-minute dedup window — same (topic, project_id) pair within 5 min is suppressed
CRITICAL escalation policy — 15-minute acknowledgement window before auto-escalation
Metrics extraction — latency percentiles, p95, and aggregation from traces
percentile() and latency_summary() — pure stdlib stats without NumPy
MetricsSummary aggregation from any Iterable[Event]
Prometheus metrics exposition format for Grafana/Prometheus integration
The span processor pipeline — enrichment, redaction, and custom processors
Head-based sampling → BatchSpanProcessor → multi-exporter fan-out
Writing a custom processor: on_start(), on_end(), and attribute enrichment patterns
Redaction processors — complementing the built-in RedactionPolicy at the span level
Debug tooling — print_tree, visualize(), Gantt timelines, and the trace store
print_tree() for hierarchical span display in development
visualize() — generating a self-contained HTML Gantt timeline from any trace
The ring-buffer trace store — opt-in, size-bounded, and security-hardened
Part 5
compliance
Compliance engineering — from evidence to attestation
chapters
~16 hrs
est. time

This part bridges the gap between code and regulatory compliance. It covers the five major frameworks SpanForge maps to, the Compliance Evidence Chain that packages evidence for auditors, and the T.R.U.S.T. scorecard that makes compliance visible to non-technical stakeholders.
EU AI Act — Articles 9, 10, 12, 13, 14, 15 and what they require technically
compliance
NIST AI RMF — GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE as engineering controls
compliance
ISO/IEC 42001 and ISO 27001 — AI management systems and Annex A.12.4
compliance
SOC 2 Type II — CC6, CC7, CC9 and how audit logs satisfy them
compliance
The Compliance Evidence Chain — building a signed ZIP bundle auditors can verify
compliance
HMAC-signed PDF attestations — what goes in them and how they are verified
compliance
RFC 3161 trusted timestamps — cryptographic proof of existence for legal proceedings
compliance
GDPR Article 30 Records of Processing — auto-generating RoPA from audit metadata
compliance
The T.R.U.S.T. scorecard — Transparency, Reliability, UserTrust, Security, Traceability
compliance
Human-in-the-Loop — EU AI Act Article 14, HITL queues, SLA tracking, escalation
compliance
Chapter details
EU AI Act — Articles 9, 10, 12, 13, 14, 15 and what they require technically
Article 9 Risk Management → PRRI scoring and gate pipeline integration
Article 14 Human Oversight → HITL queue, SLA tracking, and escalation evidence
Article 15 Accuracy and Robustness → drift detection and regression testing as compliance controls
NIST AI RMF — GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE as engineering controls
Mapping GOVERN to policy-as-code gate YAML and audit schema registry
MAP: risk identification through secrets scanning and PII detection in production
MEASURE: T.R.U.S.T. scorecard dimensions as quantified RMF measurement controls
ISO/IEC 42001 and ISO 27001 — AI management systems and Annex A.12.4
ISO/IEC 42001 Clauses 6.1, 8.3, 9.1, 10 — what they require and how CEC satisfies them
ISO 27001 Annex A.12.4.1–4 — event logging, log protection, admin logs, clock sync
NTP drift warning at 1 second — why clock synchronisation is a compliance control
SOC 2 Type II — CC6, CC7, CC9 and how audit logs satisfy them
CC6 Logical and Physical Access — API key scoping and IP allowlist as evidence
CC7 System Operations — audit chain completeness as monitoring evidence
CC9 Risk Mitigation — gate pipeline pass/fail history as risk treatment evidence
The Compliance Evidence Chain — building a signed ZIP bundle auditors can verify
ZIP structure: manifest.json, clause_map.json, chain_proof.json, attestation.pdf, rfc3161_timestamp.tsr
HMAC-signing the manifest — how auditors verify the bundle without trusting the platform
build_bundle() orchestration — sf_audit.export() → analysis → ZIP → sign → upload
HMAC-signed PDF attestations — what goes in them and how they are verified
ComplianceEvidencePackage — the analysis and PDF generation engine
Clause satisfaction status: SATISFIED, PARTIAL, GAP — and the evidence records behind each
Standalone verification — how a third-party auditor verifies the PDF without SpanForge
RFC 3161 trusted timestamps — cryptographic proof of existence for legal proceedings
Why timestamps from the platform itself are insufficient for legal proceedings
RFC 3161 TSA response format — what the .tsr file contains
DigiCert and Sectigo as public TSAs — integration patterns
GDPR Article 30 Records of Processing — auto-generating RoPA from audit metadata
What a Record of Processing Activities must contain under Article 30
generate_article30_record() — pulling processing metadata from audit records
JSON and PDF export formats for different regulatory submission contexts
The T.R.U.S.T. scorecard — Transparency, Reliability, UserTrust, Security, Traceability
Five dimensions: what each measures and how it is scored 0–100
Weighted average configuration and how to calibrate weights for different risk profiles
T.R.U.S.T. badge SVG — ETag cache-busting and shield.io-style colour bands
Human-in-the-Loop — EU AI Act Article 14, HITL queues, SLA tracking, escalation
HITLQueue as a first-class compliance module — not a workflow tool
SLA tracking and escalation — what happens when a review exceeds its time budget
How HITL review events are emitted into the HMAC audit chain as compliance evidence
Part 6
advanced
SDK architecture — building services the right way
chapters
~18 hrs
est. time

This is the engineering education core of the curriculum. The patterns SpanForge uses — circuit breaker, sliding window rate limiter, zero-dependency design, namespace schemas, plugin discovery, auto-instrumentation — all transfer directly to any SDK or platform library.
The SFServiceClient base class — retry, circuit breaker, auth refresh, fallback
advanced
Circuit breaker pattern — failure thresholds, OPEN/CLOSED/HALF-OPEN states
advanced
Sliding window rate limiting — per-key counters without external dependencies
advanced
Local fallback architecture — graceful degradation when services are unreachable
advanced
Zero required dependencies — designing with stdlib-only constraints
advanced
The namespace schema system — 19 namespaces, dataclasses, and RFC-grounded design
advanced
The plugin system — entry-point discovery and third-party scorer registration
advanced
Auto-instrumentation — monkey-patching 8 providers without breaking their APIs
advanced
The prompt registry — versioning, A/B routing, and reproducible audit trails
advanced
Model registry — lifecycle tracking (active → deprecated → retired) with HMAC events
advanced
Schema migration — v1 → v2 event migration and the MD5 rehash pattern
advanced
The CI/CD gate pipeline — YAML-driven pass conditions, 6-gate architecture, SLSA
advanced
Chapter details
The SFServiceClient base class — retry, circuit breaker, auth refresh, fallback
Exponential backoff with jitter — why constant-interval retry is wrong
Token refresh pipeline — checking X-SF-Token-Expires before every request
Local fallback flag — how the same client serves both local and remote mode
Circuit breaker pattern — failure thresholds, OPEN/CLOSED/HALF-OPEN states
The three-state model: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (failing fast), HALF-OPEN (testing recovery)
5 failures → OPEN, 30s auto-reset — choosing the right thresholds for your system
Per-exporter vs. per-service circuit breakers — different granularities for different needs
Sliding window rate limiting — per-key counters without external dependencies
Sliding window vs. fixed window — why sliding windows are fairer under burst load
Pure stdlib implementation using collections.deque — no Redis required
Returning X-SF-RateLimit-* headers — the RFC-6585 pattern for communicating limits
Local fallback architecture — graceful degradation when services are unreachable
Eight service fallback implementations — each one a documented contract
Enterprise mode: local_fallback.enabled=false raises SFServiceUnavailableError immediately
Fallback telemetry counters — counting fallback activations as a Prometheus metric
Zero required dependencies — designing with stdlib-only constraints
Why zero required dependencies is the correct default for SDK design
Optional extras pattern: pip install spanforge[presidio], spanforge[pydantic]
Graceful feature degradation — full functionality without optional packages
The namespace schema system — 19 namespaces, dataclasses, and RFC-grounded design
@dataclass with __post_init__ validation + to_dict() + from_dict() — the consistent contract
RFC clause numbers in validation comments — tracing every constraint to its specification
GuardPayload stores only content_hash (SHA-256) — compliance-by-design in the schema
The plugin system — entry-point discovery and third-party scorer registration
Python entry points — how spanforge.scorers group enables third-party eval plugins
The importlib.metadata API split between Python 3.9 and 3.10+ — and the fix
Plugin isolation — a broken plugin does not silence other plugins
Auto-instrumentation — monkey-patching 8 providers without breaking their APIs
importlib.util.find_spec before patching — never patch what is not installed
Try/except isolation — one broken integration does not silence others
The sentinel attribute pattern — preventing double-patching on repeated auto.setup() calls
The prompt registry — versioning, A/B routing, and reproducible audit trails
register(), render(), list_versions() — the minimal prompt management API
Version change events emitted into the HMAC chain — every prompt change is auditable
A/B routing between prompt versions — traffic splitting without external infrastructure
Model registry — lifecycle tracking (active → deprecated → retired) with HMAC events
model_registry.registered, model_registry.deprecated, model_registry.retired event types
Thread-safe in-memory registry with HMAC-signed lifecycle events
How model lifecycle tracking satisfies EU AI Act Article 9 risk management requirements
Schema migration — v1 → v2 event migration and the MD5 rehash pattern
Why schema migrations in event systems are harder than database migrations
The v1 → v2 migration path — field renames, added required fields, format changes
MD5 rehash migration — re-signing legacy events with the current HMAC scheme
The CI/CD gate pipeline — YAML-driven pass conditions, 6-gate architecture, SLSA
Gate YAML schema — id, type, command, pass_condition, on_fail, timeout_seconds, skip_on
_SAFE_VALUE_PATTERN and shlex.split() — template injection prevention in gate commands
SLSA Level 2 provenance — Sigstore/cosign, Rekor transparency log, CycloneDX SBOM
Part 7
advanced
Enterprise patterns — production at scale
chapters
~14 hrs
est. time

Enterprise deployments have requirements that don't appear in development environments: tenant isolation, data residency, encryption key management, air-gap deployment, and formal security review processes. This part covers all of them with concrete implementation patterns.
Multi-tenancy — project isolation, HMAC chain scoping, cross-project query guards
advanced
Data residency — EU, US, APAC, India routing and enforcement at the SDK layer
advanced
Encryption at rest — AES-256-GCM, envelope encryption, and cloud KMS integration
advanced
mTLS and FIPS 140-2 mode — certificate rotation and algorithm restriction
advanced
Air-gap deployment — offline mode, Docker Compose self-hosted stack, Helm chart
advanced
OWASP API Security Top 10 — each category as a code-level checklist
advanced
STRIDE threat modelling — applied to the 8 SpanForge service boundaries
advanced
Supply chain security — CycloneDX SBOM, Sigstore/cosign, SLSA provenance
advanced
Chapter details
Multi-tenancy — project isolation, HMAC chain scoping, cross-project query guards
(org_id, project_id) composite key as the isolation boundary
Per-project org_secret — cross-chain collusion is cryptographically impossible
cross_project_read scope — explicit authorisation required for cross-project queries
Data residency — EU, US, APAC, India routing and enforcement at the SDK layer
data_residency enum: eu | us | ap | in | global
SDK-layer enforcement — data_residency is checked before every write, not just at config time
DPDP Act data localisation requirements for IN-scoped projects
Encryption at rest — AES-256-GCM, envelope encryption, and cloud KMS integration
AES-256-GCM — authenticated encryption, nonce management, and tag verification
Envelope encryption: DEK encrypted with PMK stored in AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault / GCP KMS
Key rotation without re-encrypting all data — the DEK/PMK separation pattern
mTLS and FIPS 140-2 mode — certificate rotation and algorithm restriction
mTLS configuration: tls_cert_path, tls_key_path, tls_ca_path
PKCS#12 certificate reload without downtime
FIPS 140-2 mode — algorithm restriction at startup and what it disables
Air-gap deployment — offline mode, Docker Compose self-hosted stack, Helm chart
offline=true — what this prevents and what still works locally
Docker Compose self-hosted stack — internal networking, health checks, service dependencies
Helm chart structure — Secret template, HPA, PodDisruptionBudget, NetworkPolicy
OWASP API Security Top 10 — each category as a code-level checklist
Broken Object Level Auth → row-level filtering at the SDK layer
Broken Auth → constant-time comparison, brute-force lockout, token revocation lists
Excessive Data Exposure → SecretStr, content hashing, never raw text in audit records
STRIDE threat modelling — applied to the 8 SpanForge service boundaries
Spoofing → API key format validation, IP allowlists, JWT jti revocation
Tampering → HMAC chain, WORM storage, fsync semantics
Repudiation → Actor context, session activity log, non-repudiable audit chain
Supply chain security — CycloneDX SBOM, Sigstore/cosign, SLSA provenance
CycloneDX 1.5 SBOM — what to include and how to attach it to a release
Sigstore keyless signing — cosign sign-blob, Rekor transparency log, and verification
VEX statements — documenting not_affected CVEs with justification in the SBOM
Part 8
testing
Testing, DX, and shipping production SDKs
chapters
~12 hrs
est. time

The test suite and developer experience layer of SpanForge are as carefully designed as the production code. This part covers the testing patterns, toolchain configuration, and DX investments that make the SDK usable and verifiable by teams other than the one that built it.
Property-based testing with Hypothesis — strategies, settings, and invariant design
testing
The conformance suite — portable JSON fixtures, clause IDs, and third-party verification
testing
The mock layer — mock_all_services(), call recording, and test isolation
testing
Sandbox mode and the doctor CLI — zero-network local development
testing
Type stubs (.pyi) — writing stubs that make mypy strict and IDEs happy
testing
Toolchain setup — ruff, mypy strict, bandit, pip-audit, pre-commit, hatchling
testing
Writing SDK examples that actually teach — the production_multi_agent.py pattern
testing
Chapter details
Property-based testing with Hypothesis — strategies, settings, and invariant design
@given, @settings, and @example — the three decorators you need to know
Designing strategies: constrained not random — why st.text() alone is wrong for most cases
The ULID monotonicity property test — finding threading bugs Hypothesis catches and unit tests miss
The conformance suite — portable JSON fixtures, clause IDs, and third-party verification
17 conformance cases — C001–C017 and what each proves
Fixture JSON structure: clause_id, input, expected_output, description
How a third-party implementation uses the fixtures to prove RFC-0001 compliance
The mock layer — mock_all_services(), call recording, and test isolation
mock_all_services() as a context manager — patching all 11 sf-* singletons in one line
Call recording — asserting that sf_audit.append() was called with the right schema key
Why MockSFIdentity must match SFIdentityClient — the mock is the contract
Sandbox mode and the doctor CLI — zero-network local development
SF_SANDBOX=true — all service calls routed to in-memory local implementations
spanforge doctor — config valid, services reachable, key not expired, patterns loaded
The spanforge doctor output format — coloured pass/fail for each check
Type stubs (.pyi) — writing stubs that make mypy strict and IDEs happy
When to write .pyi stubs vs. inline annotations — the tradeoffs
Stub coverage for all 11 SDK clients — what mypy strict mode actually requires
Common stub mistakes: missing overloads, wrong return types for context managers
Toolchain setup — ruff, mypy strict, bandit, pip-audit, pre-commit, hatchling
ruff rule selection: S (bandit), B (bugbear), PD (pydocstyle), PERF (perflint) — why each rule set matters
mypy strict=true — every flag it enables and what each one catches
filterwarnings = error in pytest — catching ResourceWarning and DeprecationWarning in CI
Writing SDK examples that actually teach — the production_multi_agent.py pattern
What makes an example instructive vs. a toy — the production_multi_agent.py criteria
Showing orchestrator → sub-agent trace hierarchy in a single file
secrets.token_bytes(32) + base64 in examples — teaching correct key generation by default
